text: A very short introduction to the cosinor methods is given. They are based on considering the analysis of time series in chronobiology as a regression problem. This is possible even if the time series does not consist of equidistant data points. In addition, the prior knowledge or the external information (in the form of known periods for which confidence intervals for the amplitudes, acrophases and mesor of the corresponding components should be derived) can be fully used in this approach. The extended cosinor method, which is designed to enhance the detection of rhythms and the corresponding characteristics in data series, originally deals with short and relatively sparse data series, but it can be applied also to longer data series. Some assumptions and methods concerning these have to be considered. Finally some examples of applications are given, which start with the basic rhythms investigated by means of the original (Basic) Cosinor Method and proceed up to the description of so-called chronomes representing the mapping of very broad time structures.
explanation: The passage reads like a concise, domain-specific scientific overview with standard terminology; it could be human-written, though it also resembles AI-generated text that preserves technical accuracy.
